title: Legalised Corruption: The Case Against Lobbying in America
subtitle: Every year, billions of dollars flow from corporations and interest groups into the American political system. It is entirely legal. That is exactly the problem.
excerpt: Every year, billions of dollars flow from corporations and interest groups into the American political system. It is entirely legal. That is exactly the problem.

bodyBlocks.text: American lobbying is often defended as a form of free speech. In practice, it has become an industry that allows those with the most money to purchase privileged access to lawmakers, shape regulations in the shadows and dilute the meaning of democratic representation.

This essay explores how lobbying works in practice, why it functions as a form of legalised corruption, and what it reveals about the weaknesses of American democracy. It is not an argument against persuasion or advocacy as such, but against a system that systematically amplifies some voices while muting others.
How Lobbying Really Operates

Lobbying is not just a matter of sending position papers or organising one‑off meetings with elected officials. It relies on permanent relationships between corporate interests and the political class: former legislators who pass through the \
into consultancy firms; think tanks funded by opaque donors; and legal strategies designed to stretch the limits of campaign finance rules.
In theory, every citizen has an equal right to petition their representatives. In reality, only a small group can afford to make their petitions impossible to ignore.
Legal, But Not Legitimate

Defenders of the status quo often insist that because lobbying is regulated, it cannot be corruption. But corruption is not only a matter of envelopes of cash exchanged in back rooms. It is also the gradual bending of public institutions towards private priorities, even when every rule is technically respected.
To take lobbying seriously is to recognise that the problem is not a few \
but an entire architecture of influence. So long as access to power is structured around money, the promise of equal citizenship will remain fragile.

bodyBlocks.title: Born Into It: Why Crime Follows Poverty, Not Race
bodyBlocks.subtitle: The question is not why Black Americans and immigrants are overrepresented in crime statistics. The question is why we keep avoiding the actual answer.
bodyBlocks.excerpt: Across the United States and France, crime statistics are routinely cited alongside racial and ethnic breakdowns — as if proximity were argument. It isn't. What produces crime is poverty, spatial exclusion, and the deliberate removal of legitimate pathways to stability. When you control for those variables, the racial disparities collapse.

bodyBlocks.text: There's always been the debate concerning criminality and black people or immigrants. Now, more than ever, the debate has been central with the rise of MAGA Far-Right, and the french far right in France. Although that is the case for many countries, let us focus on the United States and France, for they are the ones I know most. In American cable news studios, in French political debates, in comment sections and dinner tables across the western world. Fox News, CNews, BFM TV. Crime statistics are cited, racial or ethnic breakdowns are noted, and someone allows the proximity of those two facts to do the work of an argument.

Why these debates dominate television is another discussion entirely, one tied to the structure of modern media ownership, where a handful of billionaires control large segments of the news landscape, like Vincent Bolloré, Patrick Drahi or the Murdoch family. The implication, rarely stated plainly because stating it plainly would require defending it, is that certain groups are more disposed to criminality. That something in the culture, the temperament, the background, produces more criminals.

This argument is wrong. It's not just politically inconvenient. Not simply uncomfortable. Wrong. The evidence is extensive, consistent across decades and continents, and available to anyone willing to look at it honestly. What produces crime is not race, ethnicity, or national origin. What produces crime is poverty, concentrated disadvantage, spatial segregation, and the systematic removal of legitimate pathways to stability. When you control for those variables, the racial disparities in crime rates disappear or collapse dramatically.

What remains is not a story about who people are. It is a story about what was done to them, what was built around them, and what was deliberately withheld from them. And that story does not begin in the banlieue or the hood. In many cases, it begins in a colony.
The Sociology They Don't Teach You

The public debate consistently skips the sociology. It jumps straight to the statistics, reads them in isolation, draws the conclusion they seem to invite, and stops there. It does not ask what produces the statistics. It treats them as a description of people rather than as the output of a system.

Robert Merton's Strain Theory, making its first appearance in 1938, begins with a simple observation: modern society sets goals it presents as universal. In the United States, the goal is material success, upward mobility, the comfortable life. The society communicates this relentlessly, through advertising, through culture, through the premise of the American Dream. It tells everyone, regardless of where they start, that this is what they should want and what they can achieve.

But the means of achieving it are not equally distributed. For some people, the legitimate pathway runs through a decent school, a network of contacts, access to capital, a neighbourhood where the institutions function. For others, that pathway is blocked by poverty, by geography, by history. Merton's insight was that when the gap between the goal and the available legitimate means becomes too wide, people find other means. Crime is not a deviation from society. It's a rational adaptation to a society that has excluded you from its legitimate pathways while continuing to insist that you want what everyone else wants.

Edwin Sutherland developed this further with Differential Association Theory in 1939. Basically, criminal behaviour, like all behaviour, is learned. You learn it from the people around you, from what is modelled in your environment, from what is rewarded in the social world you actually inhabit. Nobody is born a criminal. The environment produces the behaviour. And the environment was constructed by someone.

Pierre Bourdieu understood the French dimension through the concept of capital, not just financial but social and cultural: the networks you can access, the institutions that recognise you as a legitimate participant. The banlieue is not simply poor. It is cut off. A place the republic built at a distance from itself, filled it with people it did not want near the centre, and then blamed for what the distance produced. But to understand how those people got there, you have to go back to where they came from, and why they left, and why the leaving was not really a choice.
The Chain That Starts Before the Immigration

France did not simply receive immigrants from Africa. France created the conditions that made emigration the only rational option for millions of people, deliberately, over more than a century, and in many respects is still doing it.

Formal colonial rule ended in 1960. The independence was largely nominal. The systems set up to exploit the colonies remained in place. What followed was Françafrique: France's informal sphere of influence maintained through economic control, political manipulation, and military intervention. Between 1960 and 1991 alone, France intervened militarily in at least sixteen African countries. When African leaders attempted to challenge this arrangement, the consequences were severe. Thomas Sankara, the president of Burkina Faso who refused the system and pursued genuine economic independence, was assassinated in 1987. Sylvanus Olympio of Togo, who wanted to establish a national currency, was assassinated in 1963.

The primary instrument of economic control was the CFA franc, used across fourteen African countries, pegged first to the French franc and later to the euro. Member states were required to deposit a significant portion of their foreign exchange reserves in a French Treasury account. They could not pursue independent monetary policy or devalue their currency to make exports competitive. French multinationals took over uranium mines in Niger, plantations in Cameroon, oil refineries in Gabon. The wealth flowed outward. The populations living above it saw little of the return.

This system produced poverty in countries of enormous natural wealth. It was designed to. And the people in those countries, impoverished by a system built to impoverish them, looked at Europe and saw what their labour had built. They came for it. They arrived in France and went to the cheapest available housing. Which was in the banlieues.

The American case follows a similar chain. The ancestors of Black Americans did not come for a better life. They were brought in chains to build someone else's. Slavery ended in 1865. What followed was not freedom in any material sense. It was a century of legal apartheid: Jim Crow laws enforcing racial segregation across every domain of public life, convict leasing systems that effectively re-enslaved Black men through criminal charges designed to produce labour, systematic exclusion from political participation, and the constant threat of racial violence the state refused to prosecute. The 13th Amendment abolished slavery except as punishment for a crime. That clause was immediately and deliberately exploited. Its legacy runs directly into the modern American prison system.
America told Black people they were free. What they were given was the word. Yet, the conditions remained.
The Geography of Exclusion
Freedom without resources is a performance. The mechanism that ensured the resources did not follow was, in America, called redlining.

From the 1930s through the 1960s, the Federal Housing Administration systematically refused to insure mortgages in Black neighbourhoods, marking them as hazardous in red. This denied Black families access to the primary wealth-building mechanism of the twentieth century American middle class: homeownership and the generational equity it produced. White families, backed by government-insured loans, accumulated wealth across generations in expanding suburbs. Black families were locked out, concentrated in urban neighbourhoods simultaneously denied investment and infrastructure. This was not the decision of individual prejudiced bankers. It was federal government policy, explicit and documented.

Then came the interstate highway system. Federal infrastructure, routed deliberately through Black neighbourhoods in city after city during the 1950s and 1960s. Communities that had survived everything and maintained social cohesion were physically demolished to make way for roads serving the suburban commuters who had already left. The people displaced were moved to public housing at the urban periphery, concentrated further, isolated further.

Research from the SAVI Institute found that historical redlining explains 62 percent of the variation in violent crime rates between American neighbourhoods today, and 53 percent of the difference in median household income. Neighbourhoods rated highest by federal assessors in the 1930s have average household incomes above $130,000 today. Neighbourhoods rated as hazardous average under $40,000. The policy ended in 1968. But the geography it created did not.

In France, the mechanism differs but the result is the same. According to INSEE (2008), 25 percent of African immigrants and 30 percent of Turkish immigrants lived in banlieues classified as Sensitive Urban Areas, compared to only 4 percent of native French. Forty-two percent of sub-Saharan African households in France fall beneath the national poverty line. Research published in the Revue française de sociologie shows that African immigrants are three times more likely than others to move into the least-advantaged neighbourhoods, and that the more disadvantaged the neighbourhood, the more its residents move into another equally disadvantaged one. This is not a transitional phase on the way to integration. It's a permanent structure.

The children of these families attend schools in these neighbourhoods. Those schools are not equivalent to schools in wealthier areas: higher student-to-teacher ratios, lower access to the classes préparatoires that lead to the grandes écoles, fewer connections to the professional networks that turn a CV into an interview. Recruitment studies in France have consistently shown that identical CVs receive significantly fewer responses when they list an address in a zone urbaine sensible or carry a name read as North African or sub-Saharan.
The War on Drugs Was Not About Drugs
Now we arrive at the part of the American story that is not a theory or an inference. It is a confession.

John Ehrlichman was Richard Nixon's domestic policy chief. In 1994, journalist Dan Baum interviewed him while researching a book on drug prohibition. Ehrlichman, after public disgrace and a stretch in federal prison, spoke with the bluntness of a man who had little left to protect. As Baum later published in Harper's Magazine in April of 2016, Ehrlichman said:

"The Nixon campaign in 1968, and the Nixon White House after that, had two enemies: the antiwar left and Black people. We knew we couldn't make it illegal to be either against the war or Black. But by getting the public to associate the hippies with marijuana and Blacks with heroin, and then criminalizing both heavily, we could disrupt those communities. We could arrest their leaders, raid their homes, break up their meetings, and vilify them night after night on the evening news. Did we know we were lying about the drugs? Of course we did." — John Ehrlichman, Harper's Magazine, April 2016

This was not a lone voice. Nixon's chief of staff H.R. Haldeman recorded in his diary that Nixon himself stated the problem was "really the Blacks" and that the key was to "devise a system that recognizes this while not appearing to" (Haldeman, cited in Parenti, Lockdown America, 1999).

The war on drugs, which has been the primary driver of mass incarceration in the United States for fifty years, was designed explicitly to target Black communities for political reasons. Not because of crime rates. Not because of public health. Because they were political enemies of the Nixon administration and the law was a weapon available to use against them.

The consequences are still running. The United States holds five percent of the world's population and twenty-five percent of its prison population. Black Americans make up thirteen percent of the population and approximately thirty-eight percent of the prison population. The incarceration rate for Black men is five times that of white men according to the Bureau of Justice Statistics (2023). The standard response is to read these numbers as evidence of cultural pathology. Ehrlichman told you what they actually are — they're the intended output of a system designed to produce exactly this result.

And the system sustains itself economically. Private prison companies like CoreCivic and GEO Group are publicly traded corporations whose revenue depends on occupancy. They lobby for longer sentences, stricter enforcement, and harsher immigration policy because empty cells cost money. Some government contracts include minimum occupancy guarantees, meaning the state pays for empty cells if it does not fill them. Mass incarceration is an industry. The law is its supply chain. And poor Black and brown communities are its raw material.
What the Numbers Actually Say

In France, 70 percent of prisoners come from the poorest 20 percent of the population according to l'Observatoire national de la délinquance. In the United States, the prison population is similarly concentrated in the lowest income brackets, and people from households earning under $22,500 are more than twice as likely to be victims of violent crime as those earning above $75,000 (Bureau of Justice Statistics, 2022).

These numbers cut across two countries with different demographics, different legal systems, and different political languages. The variable running through both is not race. It is economic marginalisation.

Robert Sampson's landmark Project on Human Development in Chicago Neighbourhoods (1997), surveying 8,782 residents across 343 neighbourhoods, found that what predicted violence was not racial composition but collective efficacy, meaning the degree of social cohesion in a community and its capacity for mutual support. Concentrated poverty destroyed that cohesion. Some Black and poor Chicago neighbourhoods had low crime rates precisely because their social fabric remained intact. The variable was the condition of the community, not the character of its residents.

A 2024 study examining crime rates across Houston zip codes found that once poverty, unemployment, and educational disadvantage are controlled for, racial disparities in crime rates reduce dramatically or disappear entirely (ScienceDirect, 2024). The variable is not who people are. It is what conditions they are living in.

There is also the question of who gets counted. Policing is disproportionately concentrated in poor and minority neighbourhoods in both countries. The same behaviour, cannabis possession for example, is far more likely to produce an arrest and a criminal record for a young man from Aubervilliers or Sarcelles than for one from the 16th arrondissement of Paris. Crime statistics therefore reflect not only the incidence of behaviour but the intensity of surveillance. Heavy policing produces more statistics, which justify more policing. The circularity of this is almost never acknowledged in public debate, where crime statistics are presented as neutral measurements rather than as outputs of a system with a specific geography of enforcement.
The Counterargument and Why It Fails
The most common serious counterargument: if poverty causes crime, why do some poor groups commit less crime than others?

The argument is not simply about income poverty. It's about the full configuration of disadvantage — poverty combined with spatial isolation, multi-generational exclusion from wealth-building, the stigma of the neighbourhood, discriminatory policing, discriminatory hiring, and the historical destruction of the institutional structures that allow communities to organise and support themselves. It's the combination and concentration of these factors over time that produces the conditions for crime, not any single variable in isolation.

Recent immigrants often arrive with high social cohesion, strong family networks, and a cultural orientation toward investment in the next generation. These are protective factors. What erodes them is precisely prolonged exposure to concentrated disadvantage across generations. William Julius Wilson described this as the threshold effect (1987). Crime does not increase linearly with poverty. It increases dramatically when poverty reaches a certain concentration, when the normal social mechanisms that moderate behaviour literally collapse. The difference between a poor neighbourhood with some employed adults and stable families and one where unemployment sits at fifty percent is not culture. It is concentration. And the concentration was produced by policy.
The Stigma That Finishes the Job
After all of the above has run its course, one final mechanism locks it in place.

When you grow up in a neighbourhood the media consistently associates with crime, when your name or your address triggers an immediate recognition that marks you as belonging to a category of risk, when the police treat you as a suspect before you have done anything, when the employer does not call back, when the teacher has already adjusted expectations downward, the effect on your understanding of what is available to you is cumulative and total. Bourdieu called this symbolic violence. It's the reproduction of social hierarchy not only through material exclusion but through the internalisation, by those excluded, of the inferiority the system assigns to them.

A young man who has been told consistently by every institution he has encountered that he is not a legitimate participant in the normal economy eventually stops presenting himself as one. This isn't a cultural failure. It's a rational response to accurate information about what your available options are. And when he finds other options, and gets caught, his story becomes a statistic that someone will cite as evidence that people like him are naturally more criminal.
The circle closes. The system produces the outcome, the outcome justifies the system, and the people inside it are never asked what built the walls.
The Full Chain

France colonised North and West Africa. It extracted the resources, restructured the economies to serve French industry, imposed a currency that kept African states fiscally dependent on Paris for decades after formal independence, backed dictators who served French interests, and eliminated leaders who pursued genuine sovereignty. This produced poverty in countries of enormous natural wealth. The people in those countries came to France for the life that had been built from their labour. They were directed to the cheapest housing, which was in the banlieues. Their children grew up in inferior schools with fewer connections to the professional world, faced discrimination at every point of contact with the labour market, and some, excluded from the legitimate economy, turned to the informal one. The crime statistics were noted. The politicians said it was a cultural problem.

America enslaved Black people for two and a half centuries, promised them freedom and delivered the word without any substance, denied them land and political participation, enforced their subordination through a century of legal terror, locked them out of the postwar wealth boom via redlining, destroyed their communities through highway construction, then launched a war on drugs that its own architects admitted was literally designed to criminalise them for political reasons. It built a prison industry that profits from incarcerating them at five times the rate of white Americans. The crime statistics were noted. The politicians said it was a cultural problem.

In both cases the chain runs from a decision made by powerful people to a statistic cited by those same people's successors as evidence that the victims of the original decision are inherently criminal. The explanation locates the problem in the character of the people at the bottom of the chain. Which is convenient for the people who built it, because a cultural explanation demands nothing from them. A structural explanation would require them to look honestly at what they built and decide whether they intend to dismantle it.
I don't think society is broken. In my opinion, it's working exactly as it was designed to work. And that is the most damning thing I can say about it.

"Others before me have said and others after me will say how the gap has widened between the wealthy and those who aspire only to eat one's fill, quench their thirst, survive and maintain their dignity. But no one can imagine how much the poor man's grain has fed the rich man's cow." — Thomas Sankara
"Those who can make you believe absurdities, can make you commit atrocities." — Voltaire
"Man cannot be freed by the same injustice that enslaved it." — Pierce Brown
"I am a person who is unhappy with things as they stand. We cannot accept the world as it is. Each day we should wake up foaming at the mouth because of the injustice of things." — Hugo Claus
